_note: Tier-4 Phase 4.3 — Pattern Catalog. Closed taxonomy of named clip-worthy interaction shapes the LLM evaluates against. User-editable; hot-reloaded each pipeline run. Each entry: id (lowercase_underscore), label (display), signature (one-paragraph definition the LLM reads), structural_signals (list of conversation_shape signals or audio/vision hints), example (illustrative — optional), category_hint (default category if pattern matches and the model doesn't override). Don't grow past ~12 patterns — the LLM needs to internalize them in one prompt; more patterns = vaguer matches.

patterns.label: Setup → external contradiction
patterns.signature: First-person assertive claim ('this is mine', 'I built this', 'trust me') followed within 30s by an off-screen voice or third party contradicting it; the original speaker concedes, laughs, or pivots. The contradiction is the moment.
patterns.example: Streamer pitches a course while bragging the penthouse is theirs; off-screen voice exposes that the penthouse belongs to someone else; streamer admits.

patterns.label: Challenge and fold
patterns.signature: Speaker A escalates with a bold claim, name-call, or insult → speaker B challenges them on it ('say that again', 'are you serious') → A backs down, walks it back, or admits fear. Three or more turns; concession is the payoff.
patterns.example: Host insults a guest in jest; guest dares host to repeat it to their face; host admits they wouldn't.

patterns.label: Reading chat → reaction
patterns.signature: Streamer pauses mid-flow to read or paraphrase a chat message, then reacts emotively — laughs, corrects, gets pissed, or deadpan-responds. The reaction is the moment, not the message.
patterns.example: Streamer reads a chat troll mid-stream and snaps back with a one-liner.

patterns.label: Storytelling arc
patterns.signature: Speaker opens with a story-marker ('let me tell you about', 'one time', 'so basically') → narrative buildup with concrete detail → punchline reveal or twist. The reveal is the moment.
patterns.example: Streamer tells a 90-second crashed-the-wrong-wedding story — clean setup, escalation, and payoff.

patterns.label: Hot take with pushback
patterns.signature: Controversial claim ('honestly, X is the only correct take') → audience or guest pushes back → speaker doubles down rather than backing off. Doubling down is the moment.
patterns.example: Streamer doubles down on a deliberately controversial pizza take, refuses to back off when chat pushes back.

patterns.label: Informational ramble
patterns.signature: Sustained monologue (>=60s) on a coherent topic with concrete substance — financial advice, news analysis, backstory revelation, motivational reflection, social-dynamics commentary. The substance is the moment, not a single payoff line. Reject vague 'opinion drift' that has no concrete claims or anecdotes.
patterns.example: Streamer rants for two minutes about why streaming platforms are structurally broken, naming specific incidents and policy changes.

patterns.label: Interview revelation
patterns.signature: Host probes guest with a pointed question → guest reveals something unexpected, candid, or self-implicating. Two or more speakers. The revelation is the moment.
patterns.example: Guest reveals on stream that they once paid for a stunt that they now regret.

patterns.label: Rap battle / freestyle
patterns.signature: Rhythmic cadence with rhyme density over a beat. Turn-taking between two or more MCs, or one streamer freestyling solo. Audience or chat reaction at punchlines. Music dominance is high; speech feels metered.

patterns.label: Social callout
patterns.signature: Speaker A makes a claim → speaker B exposes an inconsistency, names the lie, or roasts → social tension or laughter follows. Different from challenge_and_fold in that A doesn't necessarily fold — the call-out itself is the moment.
patterns.example: Friend in the stream catches the streamer in a story they told differently last week.

patterns.label: Unexpected topic shift
patterns.signature: Abrupt topic pivot mid-flow that lands — audience reacts (chat catches up, guest laughs, streamer themselves notices) and the pivot itself becomes interesting. Pure tangents that don't land are NOT this pattern.
